coredata.dc:title: Tuning conductivity and roughness of diselenide polymer dot-coated surface for ROS-mediated selective real-time wireless detection of cancer cells
coredata.dc:creator: Kim, Seul Gi
Ryplida, Benny
Giang, Nguyen Ngan
Lee, Gibaek
Lee, Kang Dae
Park, Sung Young
coredata.dc:description: Reactive oxygen species (ROS)-responsive materials for the selective and sensitive electrochemical detection of cancer cells have been studied comprehensively, especially owing to the variable concentration of H2O2 in normal and tumor cells. In this report, we develop a real-time electrochemical sensor based on diselenide-functionalized dopamine-conjugated hyaluronic acid polymer dots (PD(HA/DP)-DiSe) nanoparticles coated on a substrate that could be used for selective tumor diagnosis by tuning the coated surface morphology. The hyaluronic acid-based cancer cell-targeted PD(HA/DP)-DiSe-coated surface demonstrates selectivity and sensitivity toward ROS because of the change in the optical fluorescence after treatment. Owing to cleavage of the Se-Se bond and the formation -Se-OH/-Se-OOH, the PD(HA/DP)-DiSe coated-surface is tuned from a sphere-like to a sheet-like form with smaller size changing the electronic impedance of the sensor. A real-time in vitro selective diagnosis conducts by connecting the sensor to the wireless system and produces an accurate result to distinguish different ratios of MDCK:MDA-MB-231 and MDCK:HeLa cells. Finally, the developed ROS-responsive sensor could specifically differentiate the cancer microenvironment, which is helpful for advanced and selective biomedical purposes.

TEM images of PD(HA/DP)-DiSe e) before and f) after treatment with H2O2 (0.1 mM).

a) Static contact of PD(HA/DP)-DiSe on the silicon wafer and PET surface before and after treatment with 10 mM GSH and 0.1 mM H2O2. Nyquist plot of PD(HA/DP)-DiSe treated with b) H2O2 and c) GSH. SEM images and EDX of d-1) PD(HA/DP)-DiSe and d-2) PD(HA/DP)-DiSe after treatment with 0.1 mM H2O2, and d-3) PD(HA/DP)-DiSe after treatment with 10 mM GSH. Confocal images and deep profile of e-1) PD(HA/DP)-DiSe and e-2) PD(HA/DP)-DiSe after treatment with 0.1 mM H2O2, and e-3) PD(HA/DP)-DiSe after treatment with 10 mM GSH.

Nyquist plot of PD(HA/DP)-DiSe on the silicon wafer surface after attachment of a) MDCK and b) MDA-MB-231 cells at different density. c) Electronic resistance signals of the PD(HA/DP)-DiSe-coated surface with attached different cells density MDCK and MDA-MB-231 cells as observed via wireless connection to a smartphone. d) Confocal image and deep profile of the coated surface with the attached cells (105 cells mL−1). (Scale bar: 20 μm).

SEM images and EDX spectra of the surface after detachment of a) MDCK and b) MDA-MB-231 cells (inset is the image at 100,000 × magnification). Flow cytometry spectra of the detached c) MDCK and d) MDA-MB-231 cells from the coated surface. Narrow Scan of Se 3d XPS in the e) PD(HA/DP)-DiSe-coated surface, f) MDCK cell-detached coated surface, and g) MDA-MB-231 cell-detached coated surface.

a) Illustration of the indicator for ROS reactions between the PD(HA/DP)-DiSe-coated surface and the normal and cancer cells [process: immersion of the cell-detached coated surface in H2O2 solution (reacted H2O2 solution) → reacted H2O2 solution with PD-MnO2]. b) Photoluminescence spectra and c) confocal images of the effects of different treatments with the PD-MnO2 solution. Legend: 1. PD-MnO2; 2. PD-MnO2 with H2O2; 3. PD-MnO2 solution with the remaining H2O2 after treatment of the coated surface with H2O2 solution; 4. PD-MnO2 solution with the remaining H2O2 after treatment with the MDCK-detached coated surface (A surface) using H2O2 solution; 5. PD-MnO2 solution with the remaining H2O2 after treatment with the MDA-MB-231-detached coated surface (B surface) using H2O2 solution.

ce:title.ce:sup: Tuning conductivity and roughness of diselenide polymer dot-coated surface for ROS-mediated selective real-time wireless detection of cancer cells

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Reactive oxygen species (ROS)-responsive materials for the selective and sensitive electrochemical detection of cancer cells have been studied comprehensively, especially owing to the variable concentration of H2O2 in normal and tumor cells. In this report, we develop a real-time electrochemical sensor based on diselenide-functionalized dopamine-conjugated hyaluronic acid polymer dots (PD(HA/DP)-DiSe) nanoparticles coated on a substrate that could be used for selective tumor diagnosis by tuning the coated surface morphology. The hyaluronic acid-based cancer cell-targeted PD(HA/DP)-DiSe-coated surface demonstrates selectivity and sensitivity toward ROS because of the change in the optical fluorescence after treatment. Owing to cleavage of the Se-Se bond and the formation -Se-OH/-Se-OOH, the PD(HA/DP)-DiSe coated-surface is tuned from a sphere-like to a sheet-like form with smaller size changing the electronic impedance of the sensor. A real-time in vitro selective diagnosis conducts by connecting the sensor to the wireless system and produces an accurate result to distinguish different ratios of MDCK:MDA-MB-231 and MDCK:HeLa cells. Finally, the developed ROS-responsive sensor could specifically differentiate the cancer microenvironment, which is helpful for advanced and selective biomedical purposes.

ce:section.ce:para: Selective cancer cell sensors that utilize real-time analysis via a wireless connection to smartphones have been gaining attention recently owing to their capabilities of achieving direct diagnosis based on the reaction between the distinct cancer properties and the sensor surface.[1,2] However, scarce information regarding the cancer cell-sensor is a hindrance, limiting the efforts of researchers to gain a better and clearer understanding of such technology. Currently, rapid and reliable cancer diagnosis and analysis are required because of the fast-moving global lives and increasing awareness of healthy individuals.[3,4] Thus, there is an urgent need to gain more information regarding life-threatening diseases such as cancers. It is well known that tumor cells contain four times the glutathione (GSH) concentration compared to that in normal cells. Another factor that needs to be considered is that tumor cells have concentrated reactive oxygen species (ROS), such as H2O2, which are increased approximately 10-fold in cancer cells compared to the ROS in normal conditions, thus making it an important factor to specify the tumor condition.[5–8] Generally, high ROS levels can exceed the capacity of existing antioxidant systems, leading to oxidative stress and cell aging, disease promotion, as well as cancer initiation and proliferation.[9–13] Based on its notable difference between cell types, H2O2 concentration can help distinguish cancerous tissue from normal tissue and is believed to be one of the most useful stimuli for cancer detection.[14] Thus, by combining the current knowledge regarding cancer chemical conditions and technological approaches, real-time rapid analysis can be performed.

Carbon dots (CDs) and polymer dots (PDs) have been widely recognized as fluorescent materials for use in a wide range of applications such as drug delivery, coating materials, and anti-fouling materials.[15–18] This huge range of applications comes from the capability of CDs and PDs to be modified with many functional groups and the capability to change the properties of the modified-nanoparticles.[19,20] Interestingly, PDs act as electroconductive materials due to their rich sp2 hybridization, which mimics graphene in terms of lattice spacing and structure, establishing an important environment for electron transfer.[21–23] It has also been reported that group 16 of the periodic table, containing sulfur and selenium, can be utilized as a redox-responsive system, since these elements can react with specific malignant cancer environments.[24–26] Oxidation of disulfide or diselenide in conjugated polymer systems has been widely used, such as in drug delivery,[27] hydrogel sensors,[28] and fluorescence imaging.[29] By modifying PDs with redox-responsive functional groups such as diselenide, a hybrid nanomaterial with electronic properties and responsive behavior can be achieved.

In this study, we prepared an electrical sensor based on a diselenide-crosslinked nanoparticle that can be coated on the surface of substrates such as silicon (Si) wafers and Polyethylene Terephthalate (PET) for tumor diagnosis. The controllable reactions between the diselenide-containing PDs and ROS of cells can trigger the cleavage of diselenide (-Se-Se-) and the formation of its oxidized form (-Se-OH), thus changing the properties of the material. ROS can tune the morphology of the coated surface such that it changes from a sphere to a sheet with a change in nanoparticle size, inducing sensitive and selective cancer cell diagnosis. The designed sensor could selectively detect cancer cells through optical and electronic signals to distinguish the ROS levels between normal and cancer cells. The sensor was evaluated in vitro using a combination of MDCK/MDA-MB-231 and MDCK/HeLa cells to study its selectivity and sensitivity. Moreover, the sensor was able to perform rapid and real-time detection when connected to a wireless system, producing an accurate and precise result, which would be beneficial for cancer diagnosis in the future.

Sodium Hyaluronate (HA; MW 230 kDa), dopamine hydrochloride (DP), 2-bromoethylamine hydrobromide, N-(3-dimethylaminopropyl)-N’-ethylcarbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC), N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS), selenium, sodium borohydride (NaBH4), phosphate-buffered saline (PBS) solution, ethanol, dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO), hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) 3-(4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide (MTT), and glutathione (GSH) were bought from Sigma–Aldrich (USA). Propidium iodide (PI) and calcein acetoxymethyl ester (Calcein-AM), were purchased from Molecular Probe, Life Technologies (Invitrogen). PD-MnO2 was synthesized as previous report.[30]

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of carbonized bromide and dopamine-conjugated hyaluronic acid [PD(HA/DP/Br)]
ce:section.ce:para: Bromide and dopamine-conjugated hyaluronic acid (HA/DP/Br) was synthesized by dissolving 0.5 g HA in 2 × PBS (pH 3.5), followed by addition of EDC (0.65 mmol) and NHS (0.65 mmol). The mixture was allowed to react for 2 h to activate the HA. A 50 mg dopamine hydrochloride (50 mg) was added to solution periodically and 2-bromoethylamine hydrobromide (27 mg) was added after, stirred for 24 h in room temperature. The product then purified by dialysis method (MW cut-off = 3.5 kDa) against water overnight. The obtained powder was labeled as HA/DP/Br (% yield = 83%).

The PD(HA/DP/Br) was obtained by dissolving synthesized HA/DP/Br (0.25 g) in 10 mL deionized distillated water (DDW) in Teflon chamber and put in hydrothermal reactor at 180 °C for 4 h. The crude solution then freeze-dried, and the powder was kept for the next process.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis for diselenide-crosslinked dopamine-conjugated hyaluronic acid polymer dot (PD(HA/DP)-DiSe)
ce:section.ce:para: To obtain the diselenide-functionalized PD(HA/DP)-DiSe, 200 mg PD(HA/DP/Br) was dissolved in 20 mL DDW and heated to 60 °C . Then, 1 mL of Na2Se2 stock solution, which prepared first by reacting 10 mg NaBH4 with 40 mg Selenide (Se) in DDW for 3 h, was added to the solution. The reaction was reacted for 4 h by stirring and purified by dialysis (MW cut-off = 1 kDa) for 24 h. The obtained powder was freeze-dried (%yield = 50.4%).

The PD(HA/DP)-DiSe-coated surface was prepared by dip-coating methods of silicon wafer (Si wafer) and polyethylene terephthalate (PET) with dimensional size at 1 cm × 1 cm (length × width). A 10 mg mL−1 solution of PD(HA/DP)-DiSe was prepared, and the substrates was immersed into the solution for 12 h. The coated surface was then dried and cleaned with compressed N2 to remove the excess solvent.

To investigate the coatability of the surface, the static contact angle was investigated using a DO3210 instrument. All water contact angle was measured by carefully dropping a 2 µL water droplet on the surface of a fabricated coated surfaces and analyzed using Cecil drop method. The mean value of the contact angle was achieved by measured the contact angle of the same surface at 3 different spots.

The impedance of each sample was measured with a Corrtest Instrument at room temperature using electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) technique.[30,31] The electrochemical cell was set up with 3-electrode system, which are reference electrode (Hg/HgO), counter electrode (Pt wire), and working electrode (PD(HA/DP)-DiSe coated Si wafer), and PBS pH 7.4 was used as electrolyte. An alternating current was set at 10 mV, and the measurement frequency range is 0.1 Hz to 10 kHz. The obtained Nyquist plots were fitted using simple Randle circuit consist of a resistor and a capacitor.

To record H2O2 and GSH-treated Nyquist plots, the coated surfaces were immersed to H2O2 (0.1 mM, 0.5 mM, 1 mM, 5 mM, and 10 mM) or GSH treatment (1 mM, 5 mM, and 10 mM) before impedance analysis. For MDCK or MDA-MB-231 cells-attached surface, cells with different density (101, 102, 103, 105, and 106 cells mL−1) were growth on the surface of PD(HA/DP)-DiSe-coated Si surface and incubated for 24 h at 37 °C in a humidified atmosphere with CO2. The cells-attached coated surfaces were washed with PBS to remove unattached cells and directly measured the impedance at frequency range of 0.1 Hz ~ 10 kHz with 0 V vs OCP. Prior to measurement, the impedance of Bare Si wafer was measured as a control.

For cells confocal imaging, the PD(HA/DP)-DiSe (10 mg mL−1) was dropped to SPL 200,350 Glass Bottom Confocal Dish and the solution was evaporated. After the solution was completely removed, 105 cells mL−1 was grown on the surface and incubated for 24 h at 37 °C with a humidified 5% CO2 atmosphere. The surface then washed with PBS and imaged with LSM510 confocal laser-scanning microscope (magnification, 40 × ). For live and dead assay, the prepared cells were stained with Calcein-AM and PI before observed below CLSM.

For cell viability analysis, MDCK and MDA-MB-231 cells (105 cells mL−1) were grown on the PD(HA/DP)-DiSe-coated 96-wells plate in an incubator overnight at 37 °C with a humidified 5% CO2 atmosphere. Then, the media was removed and the MTT solution was added and further incubated for another 4 h. Moreover, the MTT solubilization solution was added and shaken for 15 min before the absorbance measurement at wavelength of 570 nm. Relative cell viability was calculated and compared to that of control cells.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical analysis of the PD-MnO2 solution with pre-treated H2O2 by cells detached PD(HA/DP)-DiSe surface
ce:section.ce:para: The PD(HA/DP)-DiSe-coated Si wafer (1 cm × 1 cm) was treated with MDCK/MDA-MB-231 cells (105 cells mL−1) and incubated for 24 h at 37 °C in a humidified atmosphere with CO2. After the incubation, the cells were detached by washing the surface vigorously using PBS pH 7.4. Moreover, the coated surface was immersed to 5 mM H2O2 solution for 1 h and an amount of pre-treated H2O2 solution was added to 1 mg mL−1 PD-MnO2 and the fluorescence was measured.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Real-time wireless connected resistance measurement of the treated coated surface
ce:section.ce:para: The PD(HA/DP)-DiSe-coated Si wafer with or without attached cells was connected to the handmade wireless device consist of microcontroller (Arduino Uno) and a Bluetooth module (AppGosu). The real-time resistance of each samples was recorded as a resistance graph during the connection between smartphone and the self-made circuit via Bluetooth connection.[24,32,33].

ce:section.ce:section-title: ROS-responsive Diselenide-functionalize Dopamine-conjugated hyaluronic acid polymer dots (PD(HA/DP)-DiSe) nanoparticles for targeting cancer cells
ce:section.ce:para: The synthesis of PD(HA/DP/Br) was carried out by modifying hyaluronic acid with the dopamine and bromine groups via EDC/NHS coupling. Then, the obtained product was placed in a hydrothermal chamber, allowing the carbonization process at a temperature of 180 °C for 4 h (Scheme 1 a). Selenium was used to conjugate the PD(HA/DP/Br), forming PD(HA/DP)-DiSe nanoparticles, which contained diselenide bonds, which are ROS and GSH-responsive functional groups. By utilizing the adhesive properties of the dopamine functional group, PD(HA/DP)-DiSe was coated onto the substrates to obtain a GSH- and ROS-responsive coated surface.[34] Before exposure to ROS and GSH, the fluorescence intensity should be low due to aggregated-caused quenching promoted by increase hydrophobicity of the PD(HA/DP)-DiSe by the diselenide bonds. However, the intensity is elevated after treatment with ROS and GSH as a result of the cleavage of diselenide bonds removing the aggregation and simultaneously change the hydrophilicity of the nanoparticles. Particularly, the morphology of nanoparticles and the surface roughness of the coated substrate are tuned during treatment enabled it to change the optical and the electronic performance of the prepared surface. In the case of in vitro analysis (Scheme 1b), the cleavage of diselenide-crosslinked polymer dots occurred as an effect of the ROS reaction between the PD(HA/DP)-DiSe nanoparticles and cells.[30] The coated surface would interact with the cells via intermolecular interaction between surface and the cells. During the process, the change of coated surface morphology happened, impacting the optical fluorescence and electronic conductivity. Moreover, treatment with MDA-MB-231 would significantly influence the properties due to high concentration of ROS and GSH, in contrast with MDCK that has negligible change.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The structure, fluorescence, and morphology of PD(HA/DP)-DiSe nanoparticles
ce:section.ce:para:
The structure of the synthesized PD(HA/DP/Br) was identified using 1H NMR, as shown in Fig. 1
a. The peaks of hyaluronic acid were confirmed at 3–4 ppm in the synthesized polymer dots matrix, and the successful attachment of the dopamine to the hyaluronic acid backbone was determined through the presence of the peaks at ~ 6.5–7 ppm, which are related to the benzene ring of the catechol moieties.[25] The UV–vis spectra of the PD(HA/DP)-DiSe nanoparticles before and after treatment with GSH and H2O2 are presented in Fig. 1 b. The peak of PD(HA/DP)-DiSe at 280 nm was attributed to the catechol moieties of the nanoparticles.[35] After treatment with GSH or H2O2, the peak remained; however, the absorbance decreased because of the increased solubility of the nanoparticles compared to that in the case without H2O2 treatment. The diminished absorbance of the solution after ROS treatment was affected by the altered hydrophobicity of the nanoparticles before and after treatment with ROS. Simply, the PD nanoparticles aggregated before cleavage due to the intramolecular and intermolecular conjugation caused by the dynamic covalent property of the diselenide bonds.[36,37] However, the diselenide bridge of the PDs disintegrated after treatment with ROS, changing the -Se-Se- bond to -Se-OH, increasing the hydrophilicity and the distribution of the nanoparticles in solution.[38–40] The fluorescence characteristics of PD(HA/DP)-DiSe with or without GSH and H2O2 treatment were also analyzed. The concentrations of GSH and H2O2 were fixed at 10 mM and 0.1 mM, respectively, to mimic the intracellular concentration of GSH/ROS in tumor cells.[8,41,42] Before treatment, the fluorescence intensity of the nanoparticles was low, at approximately 12,000 a.u., which was presumed to be a result of the fluorescence quenching effect produced by the structural aggregation promoted by the diselenide bonds (Fig. 1 c).[43] However, the fluorescence of PD(HA/DP)-DiSe increased after treatment with either 10 mM GSH or 0.1 mM H2O2 as a result of the cleavage of the disulfide bonds that diminished the aggregation. The aggregation, which was promoted by the hydrophobic interactions and inter/intramolecular interactions of the diselenide bonds, was bypassed by ROS, exposing the PDs and changing the nanoparticles to a hydrophilic state, as well as causing the recovery of the fluorescence.[44] The influence of GSH or ROS on the disintegration of PD(HA/DP)-DiSe was observed from the hydrodynamic diameter data in Fig. 1 d. Before treatment, the PD(HA/DP)-DiSe particle size was measured at 117 ± 1 nm, which was further reduced with an increase in GSH concentration (17 ± 1 nm). Otherwise, the size of PD(HA/DP)-DiSe rapidly decreased after treatment with 0.1 mM H2O2, reaching 19 ± 2 nm. To obtain the chemical information of PD(HA/DP)-DiSe, transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and scanning electron microscopy (SEM) elemental analysis were conducted. As shown in Figure S1 , carbon (C) and oxygen (O) elements were detected at high intensities with the selenide (Se) element is fairly distributed in the PD(HA/DP)-DiSe. Moreover, the effect of H2O2 treatment was observed with TEM images and revealed the reduced size of PD(HA/DP)-DiSe, which was consistent with DLS measurements (Fig. 1 e and f). To confirm the biocompatibility of the PD(HA/DP)-DiSe nanoparticles, live/dead assays were conducted via confocal imaging. The nanoparticles demonstrated excellent biocompatibility without a significant number of dead cells, as shown in Figure S2
a. This observation was also confirmed by a quantitative test using the MTT assay, in which the cell survivability reached 100%, demonstrating satisfactory cell viability for up to 12 h ( Figure S2
b).

ce:section.ce:section-title: ROS-responsive surface morphology for electronic and optical performance of the PD(HA/DP)-DiSe-coated surface
ce:section.ce:para:
To evaluate the coatability of the surface, the static contact angles of the PD(HA/DP)-DiSe-coated Si wafer and PD(HA/DP)-DiSe-coated PET surface before and after treatment with GSH and H2O2 were measured (Fig. 2 a). In Si substrates, the PD(HA/DP)-DiSe-coated surface exhibited an increase in the water contact angle, at 51°, compared to that of the control (bare substrate), which was at 40°. Nevertheless, the contact angles quickly decreased to 22° and 31° after treatment with GSH and H2O2, respectively, indicating a change in the hydrophobicity of the nanoparticles.[45] Similar trend was also observed for the PET substrate, in which the contact angles decreased from 100° to 60° and 75° after treatment with 10 mM GSH and 0.1 mM H2O2, respectively. Furthermore, the electronic properties of the PD(HA/DP)-DiSe-coated Si wafer were studied using electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) with a 3-electrode system. Various concentrations of H2O2 were employed to assess the effect of ROS on the surface, as shown in Fig. 2b. The resistance measured decreased with an increase in the H2O2 concentration for up to 10 mM (from 55.5 kΩ to 2.89 kΩ). The correlation between the resistance obtained from EIS Nyquist plot fitted with simple Randle circuit vs H2O2 ( Figure S3
a) showed decreasing of resistance when the ROS increase. This trend suggests the presence of a reaction between the surface and the ROS and, which altered the molecular properties of the former, promoted by the cleavage of diselenide bonds and the formation of the Se-O- functional group. Moreover, the GSH-treated surface demonstrated that a redox reaction also occurred, as shown in Fig. 2c and Figure S3b (55.5 kΩ to 24.5 kΩ). During the exposure of PD(HA/DP)-DiSe to GSH, GSH was oxidized to form GSSH, while selenide was reduced to -Se-H and further oxidized to form -Se-O.[46] The disintegration of diselenide bonds caused by the influence of GSH and ROS was observed through the morphological alterations, as observed from the SEM data, as displayed in Fig. 2d. In Fig. 2d-1, the PD(HA/DP)-DiSe displayed a sphere-like shape with a particle size of approximately 110 nm. After treatment with 0.1 mM H2O2 and 10 mM GSH, the sphere-like nanoparticles vanished, and a sheet-like morphology and the formation of smaller nanoparticles was observed (Figs. 2d-2 and 2d-3 ). The reaction between ROS and the PD(HA/DP)-DiSe-coated substrate allowed morphological modifications on the surface of the sensor and structural tuning of the nanoparticles. The hydrophobicity of the diselenide segment changed the hydrophobic formation of PDs into Se-OH and Se-OOH by H2O2, which induced the bridging of particles and enhanced the coated surface electronic conductivity. Additionally, EDX results showed that the nanoparticles remained intact, even after treatment with GSH/H2O2, as proven by the absence of a decrease in the carbon (C), oxygen (O), and selenide (Se) elements. The fluorescence and coatability of PD(HA/DP)-DiSe on the substrate are shown in Fig. 2e-1 . The images displayed a fully coated surface with low intensity (depth profile in Fig. 2e-1). However, the fluorescence became brighter, and the intensity increased after the reaction with GSH and H2O2 due to diselenide bond cleavage (Figs. 2e-2 and 2e-3).

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro wireless sensing of the PD(HA/DP)-DiSe-coated surface using MDCK and MDA-MB-231 cells
ce:section.ce:para:
Prior to in vitro analysis, the stability of PD(HA/DP)-DiSe-coated surface was evaluated by measuring the resistance after immersing the fabricated sensor into PBS and cell growing media solution for a range of time. Figure S4 displayed a stable reading after immersion up to 24 h (around 53–57 kΩ). Additionally, the H2O2 content of MDCK, MDA-MB-231, and HeLa cells were also measured. According to Figure S5 , the distinguish concentration was observed between normal cells (MDCK) and cancer cells (MDA-MB-231 and HeLa). As the coated-surface showed a distinguishing electronic performance after treatment with GSH and H2O2, an in vitro electrochemical sensor performance test using MDCK and MDA-MB-231 cells, which are normal and cancer cells, respectively, was conducted. Fig. 3a shows the electronic impedance of the sensor during the attachment of MDCK cells at different cell densities (101–106 cells mL−1). Initially, the sensor demonstrated a high charge transfer resistance, which decreased after the attachment of MDCK cells. The PD(HA/DP)-DiSe-coated surface treated with MDCK at 106 cells mL−1 exhibited a higher conductivity compared to that of the surface treated with a lower cell density (101–105 cells mL−1) due to a higher concentration of GSH and ROS released (55.5 kΩ to 34.4 kΩ). In Fig. 3b, the MDA-MB-231-attached sensor also showed a similar trend; however, the decrease in resistivity was much higher since MDA-MB-231 cells contained more GSH/ROS, approximately 4–10-fold, compared to the MDCK cells (55.5 kΩ to 1.99 kΩ). A considerable increase in conductivity was promoted by the cleavage of the diselenide bonds of the nanoparticles by GSH/ROS, which reduced the particle size and enhanced the contact area of the PD(HA/DP)-DiSe during electron transfer.[24,47] A higher density of MDA-MB-231 cells (106 cells mL−1) increased the conductivity significantly owing to the higher amount of GSH and ROS produced. Furthermore, the amount of living cells attached to the sensor was quantified using apoptosis assay ( Figure S6
a). The correlation graph between the amount of living cells and the measured resistance was prepared as shown in Figure S6
b. The sensitivity of the sensor was evaluated by converting the obtained resistance and cell concentration value into the logarithmic form (log |R| and log cell concentration), obtaining a linear calibration curve ( Figure S6
c and d). From the curves, the limit of detection (LOD) for the sensor was achieved to be 9 cells mL−1 for cancer cells, MDA-MB-231, indicating sensitivity toward tumor environment. To investigate the repeatability of the sensor, the measurement was repeated 10-times. As shown in Figure S7 , the sensor showed preferable results at different cells concentration for MDCK and MDA-MB-231 with negligible differences.

Real-time detection utilizing a Bluetooth connection to a smartphone from the self-made sensor apparatus was performed (Fig. 3c). The output displayed on the smartphone interface was a resistance curve related to the measured resistance of the sensor. Before treatment, the coated surface showed a high resistance owing to the absence of cell attachment to the sensor. However, the resistance curve slightly decreased after treatment with MDCK cells, which we assumed was the result of the interaction of GSH/ROS from the cells with the diselenide of the PD(HA/DP)-DiSe-coated surface. Interestingly, the MDA-MB-231-cell-treated surface demonstrated a wider gap between the initial resistance and the final resistance after attachment. This phenomenon was related to the effect of GSH/ROS on the surface, which is responsible for the cleavage of the diselenide bond of the nanoparticles on the surface of the sensor. Moreover, LED analysis was performed on the coated surface, with a variation in cell density, as shown in Figure S8
a. Both MDCK and MDA-MB-231 cells demonstrated undetectable signals at lower cell densities (101 and 103 cells mL−1) owing to fewer ROS reactions occurring on the surface. However, a blue light was observed during treatment with 105 cells mL−1, with the measured electrical power at 4.04 nW and 4.71 nW for MDCK and MDA-MB-231 cells, respectively. Furthermore, the attachment of cells to the coated surface was investigated using confocal imaging and the depth profile of the images. The binding of cells (cell density of 105 cells mL−1) to the PD(HA/DP)-DiSe-coated surface is shown in Fig. 3d. The attached cells changed the depth profile of the coated surface; the intensity of the MDCK cells was quite low, while the intensity of the MDA-MB-231 cells was higher. The difference in the intensity in the depth profiles was influenced by the fluorescence intensity of the nanoparticles after cell attachment. The increase in fluorescence intensity was related to the disintegration of the diselenide bond (-Se-Se) on the surface of the nanoparticle, which became -Se-O–H after exposure to the MDA-MB-231 cells and thereby allowed the fluorescence to recover. Higher concentrations of GSH and ROS in malignant tumor cells influenced the uptake of the particles by the cells. Additionally, a PD(HA/DP)-DiSe-coated surface with a lower cell density (101 cells mL−1) showed a similar phenomenon ( Figure S8
b). The fluorescence intensity was influenced by the cleavage of diselenide bonds caused by the different concentrations of GSH/ROS (low for normal cells and high for cancer cells). Hence, different concentration of ROS in the tumor cells become the most prominent factor for the sensor in the detection of cancer cells. As shown in Figure S9a, the impedance of the PD(HA/DP)-DiSe-coated Si wafer decreased on incubation with MDCK cells for 1–12 h. Interestingly, the impedance was further reduced when the wafer was treated with MDA-MB-231 cells for the same duration of incubation (Figure S9b). The large gap of the Nyquist plot exhibited by the MDA-MB-231-cell-treated coated surface was influenced by the higher concentrations of ROS and GSH in cancer cells compared to the concentrations in normal cells. This result was in conjunction with the real-time wireless detection by the cell-treated system, showing a greater change in the electronic signal for the cancer cells, specifically MDA-MB-231 (Figure S9c). Additionally, the difference in the electronic signals was studied using LED lamps, as displayed in Figure S9d. The attachment of cells to the coated surface was determined by examining the brightness of the lamps. The bare surface and coated surface showed a light “off” and an undetectable electronic current, showing high resistance, which was consistent with the EIS data. However, the light “on” could be recognized after a 12-h incubation with either MDCK cells or MDA-MB-231 cells, with a higher electronic current detected for the MDA-MB-231-treated PD(HA/DP)-DiSe-coated Si wafer (2.57 nW) compared to that of MDCK-treated wafer (4.69 nW).

The properties of the surface after detachment of MDCK and MDA-MB-231 cells were also investigated. As shown in Fig. 4 a, the PD(HA/DP)-DiSe nanoparticles maintained their sphere-like shape after MDCK cell detachment, demonstrating no significant tuning or cleavage of the disulfide bonds on the surface. In contrast, the coated surface treated with MDA-MB-231 cells displayed the cleaved form of the nanoparticles, indicating the presence of a redox reaction between the nanoparticles and GSH/ROS (Fig. 4b). The spherical shape of the nanoparticles (after MDCK cell treatment) may pose a hindrance for electron transfer during sensing unlike the sheet-like form (after MDA-MB-231 cell treatment). This is owing to the smaller size of the sheet-like form, which has a wider contact area, compared to the bigger spherical size that affects electron transfer more dramatically.[48] To evaluate whether the cells took up the nanoparticles during attachment, flow cytometry was performed. The detached cells were collected and the intensity at the range of the fluorescence emission of the nanoparticles was measured. Fig. 4c shows a marginal increase in the fluorescence intensity at 12 h, indicating a low uptake of the nanoparticles by the MDCK cells (13.1%). In contrast, the MDA-MB-231 cells exhibited a higher uptake of the nanoparticles (38.7%), which suggests the increase in fluorescence intensity (Fig. 4d). The increase in fluorescence was affected by the targeting behavior of HA-containing nanoparticles to the tumor cells. Malignant tumor cells such as MDA-MB-231 overexpress the receptor CD44, which is specific for HA, affecting the attachment of cells to the surface and enhancing the uptake of the nanoparticles to the cells.[49,50] To prove this principle, XPS analysis was also performed. The PD(HA/DP)-DiSe-coated surface (Fig. 4e) showed that the diselenide bond was still present (binding energy at 57 eV), in which the peaks remained intact after MDCK cell treatment (Fig. 4f). However, the diselenide peak was absent and a new -Se-O peak appeared after treatment with MDA-MB-231 cells (binding energy at 60 eV), as shown in Fig. 4g.[51] The presence of the new -Se-O- peak further confirmed the sensitivity of the PD(HA/DP)-DiSe-coated surface to tumor cells and presumably provides sufficient information for future biomedical sensing applications.

Due to the high affinity to CD44 receptor, the HA chain enabled the PD nanoparticles to internalize the cell membrane after being captured in endosomal compartments before subsequently escaping to the cytoplasm. Under distinctively high level of intracellular ROS of cancer cell in cytosol, the diselenide bond of PD(HA/DP)-DiSe was cleaved before the accumulation of the nanoparticles in the lysosomes. Therefore, the cellular uptake experiments utilizing Lysotracker were conducted to prove localization of the nanoparticles after endosomal escape.[52–54] A set of experiment including the use of three coated surfaces, HA/DP/DiSe become control, HA/DP/DiSe-DAPI as comparison, and PD(HA/DP)-DiSe, were performed. As shown in Figure S10 , HA/DP/DiSe-coated surface displayed no overlap signal between Lysotracker (red) and nanoparticles owing to the absence of fluorophore in the polymer system. On the other hand, dyes-conjugated polymer (HA/DP/DiSe-DAPI) and PD(HA/DP)-DiSe showed a high intensity of blue and green fluorescence, respectively, and exhibited an obvious merging signal between Lysotracker and the polymer. These merging images explain that the nanoparticles were localized in lysosomes after endosomal escape.[44,55–57]

A comprehensive study of the ROS reaction kinetics of the PD(HA/DP)-DiSe-coated surface was performed using PD-MnO2 after treatment of the MDCK- and MDA-MB-231-cell-detached coated surface with the remaining H2O2 solution (Fig. 5 ). PD-MnO2, an indicator of GSH and H2O2, was reported to have a low fluorescence (quenching) before treatment with GSH/ROS. In this analysis, PD-MnO2 was used to detect the remaining H2O2 in the solution.[30] When the H2O2 solution was reacted with the indicator solution with the coated surface after treatment, there was no change in the PD-MnO2 fluorescence due to the reduction of H2O2 by the diselenide bonds in the surface. After the detachment of MDCK and MDA-MB-231 cells, the coated surface was immersed in an H2O2 solution. The immersion of the remaining diselenide bonds in the PD(HA/DP)-DiSe-coated surface after MDCK cell treatment promoted the reaction between the surface and the H2O2 solution. In contrast, the fluorescence of the MDA-MB-231 cell-treated coated surface remained unchanged because the diselenide bonds of the sensor already reacted with the ROS of the cancer cells. As shown in Fig. 5b, the control test (PD-MnO2) with the coated surface exhibited selectivity towards H2O2, in which the surface remained quenched (fluorescence “off”) before treatment and increased fluorescence (fluorescence “on”) after immersion in H2O2. After reacting the H2O2 solution with the coated surface pre-treated with MDCK cells, the PD-MnO2 remained quenched from the decreased H2O2 concentration after the reaction with the diselenide bonds of the MDCK cell-detached PD(HA/DP)-DiSe coated surface (no. 4). In contrast, the PD-MnO2 that reacted with the H2O2 solution after treatment with the MDA-MB-231 cell-detached coated surface (no. 5) exhibited high fluorescence intensity because no reaction occurred between the broken diselenide bonds upon treatment with MDA-MB-231 and the H2O2 solution. These were further confirmed through the confocal microscopy images of PD-MnO2 after different treatments (Fig. 5c). Moreover, the surface roughness was investigated using atomic force microscopy (AFM), as displayed in Figure S11 . The PD(HA/DP)-DiSe-coated surface showed a rough morphology, owing to the distribution of the nanoparticles on the substrates ( Figure S11
a). After cell binding and detachment, the MDA-MB-231-cell-treated surface ( Figure S11
c) demonstrated a smoother morphology compared to the MDCK-cell-treated surface ( Figure S11
b), which was influenced by the cleavage of the disulfide bond that reduced the particle size of PD(HA/DP)-DiSe on the surface. Furthermore, the coated surface after cell detachment was studied using survey scans, the narrow scan of C 1 s ( Figure S12 ) and the Se 3d XPS, to assess the molecular changes and cleavage of the disulfide bonds.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro selectivity wireless sensor of the PD(HA/DP)-DiSe-coated surface using different ratios of MDCK and MDA-MB-231/HeLa cells
ce:section.ce:para: As the coated surface manifested selectivity and sensitivity towards ROS and GSH, in vitro cancer cell sensing was performed using MDCK cells as normal cells and MDA-MB-231 and HeLa cells as cancer cells. For the selective sensitivity between normal and cancer cells, the surface was treated with several ratios of MDCK and MDA-MB-231 cells. The surface which was treated with only MDCK cells (1:0), recorded the highest resistance (42.9 kΩ) compared to that of the other ratios (Fig. 6 a) due to the lower amount of ROS from the cells. The resistance kept declining along with the addition of the MDA-MB-231 cells to the mixture (36.7 kΩ, 31.4 kΩ, and 14.6 kΩ for the 8:2, 5:5, and 2:8 ratios, respectively), in which the lowest resistance was exhibited by the purely MDA-MB-231 cell-treated coated surface (1.93 kΩ). Real-time wireless detection showed a related result, with the surface treated with only MDCK cells showed the highest resistance, while the surface treated with MDA-MB-231 cells only exhibited the lowest resistance (Fig. 6b). An additional test using different cancer cell types was performed using the HeLa cell line, as shown in Fig. 6c. The combination of MDCK and HeLa cells showed that the decrease in resistance was dependent on the number of HeLa cells in the combination. A higher number of HeLa cells in the mixture increased the conductivity (7.9 kΩ); however, it was decreased when the HeLa ratio was low (42.9 kΩ). It was further suggested that the change in the electronic signal was influenced by the sensitivity and selectivity of the sensor towards cancer cells (Fig. 6d).

To compare the fabricated system with other PD-based biosensors, Table S1 (Supporting Information) was presented. Interestingly, most CD/PD-based sensors can be applied for cancer detection utilizing optical and electronic approaches. However, the implementation of simultaneous sensing techniques is not common recently, which has become a promising chance to develop a dual-approach cancer sensor. For instance, PD in their nanoparticles form is usually used as an optical sensor. However, the application of PD as an electrochemical sensor is still relied on metal such as copper (Cu), gold nanoparticle (Au), and palladium (Pd), even though the properties of PD are nearly identical with graphene. For this study, the optical and electronic approach can be obtained by the functionalized PD without any conjugation to conductive metal or dyes. Besides, it showed acceptable linearity at the range of 101 until 106 and the limit of detection (LOD) at a cell concentration of 9 cells mL−1. Therefore, the development of PD(HA/DP)-DiSe-coated surface for cancer detection would become an opening for further study and development of polymer dots-based sensors.

Herein, we successfully designed a wireless ROS-sensitive sensor based on a PD(HA/DP)-DiSe-coated surface that could be used for cancer diagnosis. After treatment with ROS, the fluorescence intensity shifted to a brighter emission due to the cleavage of the diselenide bond, changing the dimensional structure of the nanoparticles on the coated surface. The sensor during in vitro analysis using MDA-MB-231 cells showed a reduction in resistance from 55.5 kΩ to 36.7 kΩ upon treatment at a cell density of 101 cells mL−1. Interestingly, the resistance kept decreasing when the cells density increased up to 106 cells mL−1 (55.5 kΩ to 1.99 kΩ), which act differently with sensor-treated MDCK cells, even when the sensor treated with a high cell density at 106 cells mL−1 (55.5 kΩ to 34.3 kΩ). Moreover, the sensor showed a declining resistance during the increment of MDA-MB-231 and HeLa cells ratios in the MDCK normal cells-tumor cells mixture, indicating the sensitivity and selectivity of the sensor toward tumor cells. Additionally, the system showed the capability of real-time detection of ROS when connected to a wireless system to obtain an accurate and precise result. In the end, the developed ROS-sensitive sensor was found to be able to specifically distinguish the cancer microenvironment with excellent selectivity and sensitivity. Thus, it shows great potential for tumor diagnosis in the future.
